cataloging_subject.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas. This record describes the abstract work, not Brogyntyn MS II.1, Furnivall’s 1871 edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: No later than the sole witness, c. 1470; exact composition date unknown
catalog_summary.region: West Midlands (scribal dialect and witness context; composition place unknown)

titles.value: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware

assertion.notes: This is the established editorial title; the manuscript opening has no title or rubric.

titles.supplied_by: Digital Index of Middle English Verse
Manual of the Writings in Middle English 1050–1500, as cited by the METS edition

notes: This follows Furnivall and the U-M transcription; DIMEV normalizes the incipit as ‘Lief listeneth to me.’

assertion.notes: No inspected work-level source names an author. The U-M XML’s AUTHOR field contains the manuscript repository and shelfmark and is a cataloging-role error, not an authorship claim.

date_statements.display: No later than the sole witness, c. 1470; exact composition date unknown

assertion.notes: The work must predate or be contemporary with its sole known witness, currently cataloged [c.1470]. Harris gives the broader 1453–1500 range, but neither source establishes an exact composition year.
The date belongs to the physical witness, not independently to composition.

date_statements.display: 1871 (first complete publication identified; earlier notice and excerpts existed)

assertion.notes: Furnivall states that Madden had already brought the poem to public notice and Halliwell had quoted passages. The 1871 claim is therefore limited to the first complete publication found in this review.

places.label: West Midlands
notes: The current NLW exhibition describes the manuscript’s English works as West Midland in dialect and says Cheshire or Shropshire is consistent with the evidence; the exact compilation location remains unknown.

scope.description: Language of the sole known manuscript witness, not a proven place of composition

assertion.notes: No exact composition place is asserted. Harris calls the carrier a Cheshire household miscellany, whereas current NLW guidance presents Cheshire or Shropshire as possibilities.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML profile’s language declaration ‘Latin’ is false for this work and is not propagated. The closing ‘Amen’ does not make Latin a substantive embedded language.

form_statements.description: A stanzaic Middle English poem conventionally presented in twenty six-line stanzas. The witness itself has 119 written verse lines; Furnivall supplies bracketed line 8 to create 120 editorial line positions.

genre_statements.label: Alewife poem
notes: Harris identifies the work as one of the earliest surviving alewife poems: a tradition of women drinking, speaking bawdily, sharing knowledge, and disparaging absent husbands.

genre_statements.label: Fabliau
notes: METS classifies the text under Fabliau and places it within ‘Satire and Fabliaux in Verse and Prose.’

scope.description: The wives’ alehouse gathering and shared wine frame the sequence of speeches.

subject_statements.label: Female friendship and peer pedagogy
notes: Harris reads the wives’ shared complaints as cummarship, consolation, collective knowledge-making, and sexual education while retaining the poem’s misogynist framing.

summaries.value: An eavesdropping narrator reports a gathering of ten wives at an alehouse with no men present. Over wine, the wives take turns describing their husbands’ genitalia and sexual inadequacies through curses, comparisons, and explicit comic complaint. The poem combines fabliau and social satire with a scene of female fellowship, shared sexual knowledge, and consolation, even as its male-framed representation draws on misogynist stereotypes of women’s drunken and unruly speech.

The U-M XML encodes one complete poem from Furnivall pages 29–33, plus an edition title page and Furnivall’s work-level notes. It contains 20 LG elements and 120 L elements, but one L is Furnivall’s bracketed supplied line 8; the manuscript therefore has 119 physically written verse lines. The XML’s Latin language declaration and English-prose subject heading are inherited cataloging errors.

extent.description: One complete poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas and represented by 120 XML L elements. The physical manuscript contains 119 written verse lines: Furnivall supplied bracketed line 8, ‘For to singen us amonge,’ to regularize the second stanza. Five PB elements cover printed pages 29–33; seven folio milestones map the poem to Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v.

content_parts.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
content_parts.summary: The complete poem: an alehouse frame followed by the ten wives’ sequential complaints and a closing Amen.
notes: The verse_lines metric follows the conventional edited structure and XML line positions. It must not be read as a count of physical manuscript lines; the witness has 119 written lines because line 8 is supplied.
The poem begins at the top of f. 56v and ends partway down f. 59v; the remainder of f. 59v begins an adjacent lover’s complaint.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/tenwives.xml, header and source description.
evidence.locator: FILEDESC, TITLESTMT, PUBLICATIONSTMT, SOURCEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, and PROFILEDESC
evidence.summary: The header identifies CME item tenwives/AEH6189, IMEV 1852, a 2003 U-M encoding of Furnivall’s 1871 pages 29–33, and Brogyntyn MS II.1 (formerly Porkington 10). It also contains a false Latin language declaration, a false English-prose subject heading, and a legacy redistribution restriction.

evidence.citation: Computed structural and textual inspection of CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/tenwives.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: The XML has one poem, 20 stanza LG elements, 120 L elements, five PB elements for printed pages 29–33, and seven manuscript-folio milestones from 56v through 59v. It includes Furnivall’s bracketed supplied line 8 and italic/superscript markup for editorial expansions while retaining explicit sexual complaint, the alehouse frame, wine, ten sequential wives, and the closing Amen.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces (London, 1871), as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: Furnivall prints the poem on pages 29–33 from Porkington MS 10, supplies line 8 in brackets, and states that Madden had earlier publicized the work and Halliwell had quoted passages.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1 catalog record and detailed contents, as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: The current catalog dates the 212-folio miscellany [c.1470], places the unique text at fols. 56v–59v, supplies the title [The Tale of Ten Wives], records six-line stanzas and Furnivall pages 29–33, and identifies IMEV 1852.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, ‘A Middle English Miscellany,’ as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: NLW describes the English texts’ dialect as West Midland and says the compilation place is unknown, with Cheshire or Shropshire consistent with the linguistic evidence.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales complete IIIF facsimile of Brogyntyn MS II.1, canvases 114–120, as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: Direct inspection confirms the untitled opening with a large red initial on f. 56v, the absence of Furnivall’s supplied line 8 from the written witness, red paragraph initials through the poem, and the closing Amen partway down f. 59v before the adjacent work begins.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 3073, as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: DIMEV record 3073 links IMEV/NIMEV 1852 to ‘The Ten Wives Tales,’ describes twenty six-line stanzas, supplies the two exact subject terms, and records one witness at Brogyntyn II.1, fols. 56v–59v.

evidence.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Trials and Joys of Marriage (2002), ‘A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware,’ as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: METS classifies the work as Middle English poetry, fifteenth century, and Fabliau; records the manuscript and Furnivall edition; prints the complete text and notes; cites the alternate title Gossips’ Meeting; and states that line 8 was presumably added by Furnivall to maintain the six-line stanza.

evidence.citation: Carissa M. Harris, ‘Cultivating Cummarship’ (2022), as documented in lineage:tenwives.
evidence.summary: Harris identifies the poem as one of the earliest surviving alewife poems and analyzes its alehouse setting, female fellowship, bawdy complaint, sexual pedagogy, collective knowledge-making, and misogynist framing.

open_questions.question: Can the poem’s composition be dated more narrowly than ‘by circa 1470’ without turning the sole witness’s date or Harris’s broad 1453–1500 range into an exact work date?
next_steps: Consult current poem-specific scholarship and manuscript dating studies.
Keep composition, first attestation, and witness production as separate claims.

open_questions.question: Is there reliable evidence for a place of composition distinct from the sole witness’s West Midland dialect and possible Cheshire-or-Shropshire compilation context?
next_steps: Review current dialectal and codicological scholarship devoted to Brogyntyn MS II.1.
Retain West Midlands only as witness/dialect context until composition evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Can any authorial attribution be supported beyond anonymous authorship?
next_steps: Consult current work-specific scholarship for attribution arguments.
Do not treat the manuscript repository string in the U-M AUTHOR field as a person or author.

notes: Furnivall and later editors are lineage-layer contributors and are not entered as editors of the abstract medieval work in catalog_summary.
The sole witness is currently cataloged c.1470; the work’s exact date and place of composition remain unknown.
The 120-line extent is an editorial/encoding count. The physical manuscript has 119 written verse lines because Furnivall’s bracketed line 8 is supplied.
This work contains explicit sexual language and derogatory gender stereotypes; the abstract reports those features rather than sanitizing or endorsing them.
The manuscript’s West Midland dialect and possible Cheshire-or-Shropshire compilation context are not transferred into an exact composition-place claim.
